hook: You already know how to plot a single location on a graph — just drop a dot at the right coordinates. But what happens when you connect two dots, or stretch that connection forever in both directions? And what about a perfectly flat surface that extends without end in every direction? Geometry has precise names and rules for all of these, and without them you cannot describe shapes, angles, or space itself. So here is the question: what is the difference between a line segment, a line, and a ray — and why does it matter?
concept: In geometry, the three most fundamental building blocks are a point, a line, and a plane. A point is an exact location in space with no size at all — just a position. A line is a perfectly straight path that extends forever in both directions, containing infinitely many points. A plane is a flat surface that extends forever in all directions, like an endless tabletop. Formally, a point is denoted by a capital letter such as $A$, a line through points $A$ and $B$ is written $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$, and a plane is named by three non-collinear points or a single capital letter.
anatomy: Each building block has its own notation and properties worth naming carefully. A point has no dimension — no length, no width, no depth — and is labeled with a single capital letter like $P$. A line has one dimension (length) and is indicated by two points on it plus an arrow on each end, written $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$; it can also be named with a single lowercase letter like $\ell$. A line segment is the finite piece of a line between two endpoints, written $\overline{AB}$, and it has a measurable length. A ray starts at one endpoint and goes forever in one direction, written $\overrightarrow{AB}$, where $A$ is the starting point. A plane has two dimensions and is represented by a flat shape (often drawn as a parallelogram) labeled with three non-collinear points such as plane $ABC$ or a single italic capital like plane $M$.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Identify: a dot labeled } Q \text{ on a diagram}
worked_example.description_latex: We start by recognizing the simplest object — a single dot with a capital-letter label. Because it has no size, only a location, $Q$ is a point. Anytime you see a labeled dot in geometry, your first instinct should be to call it a point.

Now suppose two points $A$ and $B$ are shown, and arrows extend from both sides of the figure. The double-headed arrow above the two letters tells us this object goes on forever in both directions, so it is a line, specifically line $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$. We could also call it line $\overleftrightarrow{BA}$ — the order of the letters does not change which line it is.

Compare two figures: one has a dot at $C$, passes through $D$, and has an arrow only on the $D$ side — that is ray $\overrightarrow{CD}$, starting at $C$ and going forever toward and past $D$. The other has dots at both $C$ and $D$ with no arrows — that is segment $\overline{CD}$, a finite piece with two endpoints. The notation difference (arrow vs. bar) is exactly what distinguishes them.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Plane } ABC, \text{ where } A, B, C \text{ are non-collinear}
worked_example.description_latex: Three points that do not all lie on the same line determine exactly one plane — this is why we need three non-collinear points to name a plane. If $A$, $B$, and $C$ were all on one line (collinear), they could fit in infinitely many planes, so the name would be ambiguous. Using three non-collinear points pins down a unique flat surface.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Figure shows: dot at } P, \text{ passes through } Q, \text{ arrow on the } Q\text{-side only}
partial_example.description_latex: Look at the figure carefully. There is a filled dot (endpoint) at $P$, the path passes through $Q$, and there is an arrow only on one side. We need to decide what geometric object this is and write its name using correct notation.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{This is a } \underline{\hspace{2cm}} \text{ because it has one endpoint and extends forever in one direction.}
partial_example.description_latex: Name the geometric object and explain your reasoning — is it a line, a segment, or a ray? Think about how many endpoints you see and whether either end has an arrow.

The correct answer is ray $\overrightarrow{PQ}$. Notice that the endpoint $P$ is always written first in the notation, and the single arrow above points toward $Q$ to show the direction the ray travels forever. Writing $\overrightarrow{QP}$ would describe a completely different ray starting at $Q$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Name the geometric object: a single dot labeled } M.
\text{Write the correct notation for the line passing through points } R \text{ and } S.
\text{A figure shows a dot at } X, \text{ passes through } Y, \text{ and has arrows on BOTH ends. Name this object and write its notation.}
\text{Points } A(0,0),\, B(3,0), \text{ and } C(0,3) \text{ are plotted on a coordinate grid. (a) Are they collinear? (b) Do they determine a unique plane?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{(a) No, they are not collinear. (b) Yes, three non-collinear points determine exactly one plane.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A laser pointer starts at point } L \text{ and shines through point } K \text{ and continues forever. A piece of tape marks the segment from } L \text{ to } K. \text{ Write the notation for (a) the laser beam and (b) the taped segment.}

common_mistakes: Students write the endpoint second in ray notation — for example writing $\overrightarrow{BA}$ when $A$ is the endpoint, instead of $\overrightarrow{AB}$. The endpoint is always listed first because a ray starts there; reversing the letters describes a completely different ray going the opposite direction.

Students confuse 'collinear' with 'coplanar' and claim that three points on the same line cannot lie in a plane. In fact, any set of collinear points lies in infinitely many planes, which is exactly why three collinear points cannot name a unique plane — you need three non-collinear points to pin down exactly one.

Students use segment notation $\overline{AB}$ when they mean line notation $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$, forgetting that a segment has two endpoints and finite length while a line has neither. Always check whether the figure shows arrows (line or ray) or filled dots with no arrows (segment) before writing the notation.
untested_variants: Identifying and naming intersecting lines and the single point they share (intersection of two lines).
Determining whether a given set of points is coplanar versus non-coplanar in a three-dimensional figure such as a rectangular prism.
Using the Segment Addition Postulate: if $B$ is between $A$ and $C$ on $\overline{AC}$, finding a missing length given $AB + BC = AC$.
Writing and interpreting the notation for opposite rays — two rays $\overrightarrow{BA}$ and $\overrightarrow{BC}$ that share endpoint $B$ and form a line.
